user_text: create a complete lesson that explains some high level operations and definitions of "class" in python.

messages.content:
You are an expert autonomous Task Orchestrator.
Your goal is to analyze user requests and educational materials, then create a structured execution plan.

Available Agents:
- 'coder': Generates runnable Python code. Best for demonstrations, data analysis, and coding tutorials.
- 'video': Creates video content. Best for visual explanations.
- 'presentation': Generates PowerPoint slides (.pptx). Best for structured lesson plans, summaries, and educational materials.
- 'quizzer': Creates quizzes. Best for assessments and review.

Instructions:
1. Analyze the user's input and provided files.
2. Break down the goal into logical subtasks.
3. Assign each subtask to the most appropriate agent.
4. Provide specific, actionable instructions for each agent.
5. For every subtask, include a non-empty 'acceptance_criteria' list.
   - Each criterion must include: criterion_id, criterion_type, target, operator, expected, severity.
   - Use criterion_type values: mcp_tool | output_shape | file_exists | semantic.
   - Use operator values: equals | contains | exists | gte | lte.
   - Use target paths that start with 'result.' or 'tools.'.
   - For mcp_tool criteria, include tool: { name, args }.
   - Use placeholders like $artifacts[0] or $output.validation.path inside tool args.
6. Use the provided tool 'create_task_plan' to submit your plan.

Response Format Example:
{
  "user_intent": "Create a lesson on Python lists with a video intro and a quiz.",
  "subtasks": [
    {
      "task_id": "step1_code",
      "agent": "coder",
      "instruction": "Generate a Python script demonstrating list operations.",
      "inputs": {},
      "dependencies": [],
      "acceptance_criteria": [
        {
          "criterion_id": "code_executes",
          "criterion_type": "mcp_tool",
          "tool": {"name": "python_check", "args": {"filepath": "$artifacts[0]"}},
          "target": "tools.python_check.success",
          "operator": "equals",
          "expected": true,
          "severity": "required",
          "evidence": "tools.python_check.run.stderr"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "task_id": "step2_video",
      "agent": "video",
      "instruction": "Create a video explaining the code.",
      "inputs": {},
      "dependencies": ["step1_code"],
      "acceptance_criteria": [
        {
          "criterion_id": "video_semantic_ok",
          "criterion_type": "semantic",
          "target": "result.output",
          "operator": "exists",
          "expected": true,
          "severity": "required",
          "evidence": "result.output"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

content.text: create a complete lesson that explains some high level operations and definitions of "class" in python.

function.name: create_task_plan
function.description: Create a structured execution plan for multiple agents.

type.description: Criterion type: mcp_tool | output_shape | file_exists | semantic
type.title: Type

target.description: Evaluation target path (e.g., result.output.text or tools.python_check.success)
target.title: Target

operator.description: Operator: equals | contains | exists | gte | lte
operator.title: Operator

tool.description: Tool call definition for mcp_tool criteria
tool.title: Tool

evidence.description: Evidence path to include in judger output
evidence.title: Evidence

agent.description: The agent responsible (coder, video, text, quizzer)
agent.title: Agent

dependencies.description: IDs of tasks that must complete first
dependencies.title: Dependencies

acceptance_criteria.description: Structured criteria the judger will use
acceptance_criteria.title: Acceptance Criteria

user_intent.description: Summary of what the user wants to achieve
user_intent.title: User Intent

parsed_plan.user_intent: Create a complete lesson that explains high-level operations and definitions of a Python class.

subtasks.instruction: Draft a complete lesson plan on Python classes. Include: learning objectives, prerequisites, key vocabulary (class, object, instance, attribute, method, __init__, self, inheritance, encapsulation), conceptual explanations, and a structured flow (intro → core concepts → examples → exercises → recap). Provide speaker notes and a short glossary.

Create runnable Python code examples for the lesson illustrating: defining a class, constructor (__init__), instance vs class attributes, methods (instance and classmethod), properties, dunder methods (__repr__ or __str__), basic inheritance with super(), and a small usage demo. Include comments aligned to the lesson sections.

Write a short assessment for the Python classes lesson: 8–12 questions mixed format (MCQ, true/false, short answer, code reading). Provide an answer key and brief rationales. Ensure questions cover: class vs object, attributes vs methods, self, __init__, inheritance, and method resolution order at a high level.

Compile the final lesson document in a clean, student-friendly format (Markdown). Include: section headings, explanations, embedded code from the coder step, a guided practice walkthrough, common pitfalls, summary, and the quiz at the end. Ensure the lesson can be delivered as a 45–60 minute session.

lesson_outline_content.instruction: Deterministic failure: outline_exists is missing (no outline/lesson plan output was provided). Provide a complete lesson plan text. Ensure it explicitly includes the terms '__init__' and 'instance', and contains an 'Exercise' section. Include all requested components: learning objectives, prerequisites, key vocabulary (class, object, instance, attribute, method, __init__, self, inheritance, encapsulation), conceptual explanations, structured flow (intro → core concepts → examples → exercises → recap), speaker notes, and a short glossary.

output.code:
# Class definition example
class Person:
    # Class attribute - shared by all instances
    species = "Homo sapiens"
    
    # Constructor method - initializes new instances
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        # Instance attributes - unique to each instance
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
        self._email = None  # Protected attribute
    
    # Instance method - operates on instance data
    def introduce(self):
        return f"Hi, I'm {self.name}, {self.age} years old."
    
    # Class method - operates on class data, not instance data
    @classmethod
    def get_species(cls):
        return cls.species
    
    # Property decorator - creates getter/setter for controlled access
    @property
    def email(self):
        return self._email
    
    @email.setter
    def email(self, value):
        if "@" in value:
            self._email = value
        else:
            raise ValueError("Invalid email format")
    
    # Dunder method - string representation of object
    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.name} ({self.age} years old)"
    
    # Dunder method - official string representation for debugging
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Person('{self.name}', {self.age})"

# Inheritance example
class Student(Person):
    # Inherits from Person class
    def __init__(self, name, age, student_id):
        # Call parent constructor using super()
        super().__init__(name, age)
        self.student_id = student_id
    
    # Override parent method
    def introduce(self):
        return f"I'm {self.name}, a student with ID {self.student_id}"
    
    # Additional method specific to Student
    def study(self, subject):
        return f"{self.name} is studying {subject}"

# Usage demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Creating instances
    person1 = Person("Alice", 30)
    person2 = Person("Bob", 25)
    
    # Using instance methods
    print(person1.introduce())
    print(person2.introduce())
    
    # Accessing class attribute
    print(f"All people belong to species: {Person.species}")
    
    # Accessing class method
    print(f"Species: {Person.get_species()}")
    
    # Using properties
    person1.email = "alice@example.com"
    print(f"{person1.name}'s email: {person1.email}")
    
    # Using dunder methods
    print(str(person1))  # Calls __str__
    print(repr(person1))  # Calls __repr__
    
    # Inheritance demonstration
    student1 = Student("Charlie", 20, "S12345")
    print(student1.introduce())  # Overridden method
    print(student1.study("Python"))  # New method
    print(f"Student ID: {student1.student_id}")
    
    # Demonstrating inheritance with super()
    print(f"Student inherits from: {Student.__bases__[0].__name__}")

code_examples.instruction: Create runnable Python code examples for the lesson illustrating: defining a class, constructor (__init__), instance vs class attributes, methods (instance and classmethod), properties, dunder methods (__repr__ or __str__), basic inheritance with super(), and a small usage demo. Include comments aligned to the lesson sections.

assessment_quiz.instruction: Output must be a short assessment for Python classes with 8–12 questions in mixed formats (MCQ, true/false, short answer, code reading). Include an explicit 'Answer Key' section with brief rationales. Ensure coverage of: class vs object, attributes vs methods, self, __init__, inheritance, and method resolution order (high level). Current output has only 2 example questions and no answer key.

final_compiled_lesson.instruction: Provide the compiled lesson as a Markdown document (either as a file named final_lesson_markdown / final_lesson_markdown.md, or include the full Markdown in result.output.text). Ensure it includes: (1) at least one Python code block fenced with ```python, (2) a 'Common pitfalls' section, and (3) a 'Quiz' section at the end. Also include headings, explanations, guided practice walkthrough, summary, and ensure it fits a 45–60 minute session.
